name: ai-medication-check
title: AI Medication Check
description: Presentation surface for caller-supplied medication-review findings, grouped by severity with source, version, and explicit review actions.

props.name: findings
props.description: Findings exactly as returned by the checking service: { id, title, severity, medications?, description?, recommendation?, documentation?, evidence? }. Nothing is computed by the component.

props.name: source
props.description: Required. { name, version?, retrievedAt? } — the validated service behind the findings. A finding with no attributable source is not reviewable.

props.name: screened
props.description: The medications actually screened. Makes the coverage gap visible: anything absent here was not checked.

props.name: emptyState
props.description: Replaces the default empty copy. The default states that no findings were returned and that this is not a safety claim — override only with copy that is at least as cautious.

props.name: limitations
props.description: Overrides the default limitations line passed to AiAnswerCard.

props.name: children
props.description: Extra content rendered under the findings.

clinicalNotes: This is a display for a validated interaction-checking service, not a drug-interaction engine. It contains no clinical logic: it groups the findings the caller passes in, in the severity order the caller assigns, and it never derives, filters, or scores a finding itself. Because a finding is only as good as the knowledge base behind it, `source` is required and its name, version, and retrieval time sit in the card header — a review against a stale version is a different review, and the clinician must be able to see which one they got. The most dangerous state is the empty one. A green "No interactions found" tick reads as clearance, and clinicians act on clearance; so the empty state says instead that no findings were returned for the medications screened, that this is not a statement that the order is safe, and that the source may simply not cover the combination. `screened` exists for the same reason: a medication the service never saw cannot generate a finding, and the reviewer must be able to spot the omission. The limitations line names what the check structurally cannot see — dose appropriateness, indication, allergies, renal and hepatic function, and local protocol. All actions are caller-owned; nothing here overrides, accepts, or signs an order.
notes: Composes AiAnswerCard for the shell and takes per-finding AiEvidencePanel nodes for provenance. Severity is caller-assigned and only controls grouping and badge colour — see aiMedicationSeverities for the presentation order.
